data.title: Rubrik: A Potent Cocktail In Cybersecurity
data.description:
Introduction: A Potent Cocktail

I recently shared thoughts on the cybersecurity market, which you can read here, and Rubrik (RBRK) could be thought of as a company within a mini-ETF of cybersecurity businesses that I like.

With the complexity of cloud environments and their cybersecurity threats growing immensely with each passing day due to the advent of next-gen AI, I've never been more excited about the cybersecurity industry and the opportunities therein.

Rubrik represents yet another opportunity in the rapidly growing cybersecurity industry.

Before we work through Rubrik using my systematic approach to understanding businesses, the logic of which will seamlessly cascade as we progress, culminating in a decision to buy, sell, or hold, I'd like to share some overarching thoughts on Rubrik.

At the outset, I've not been this excited to purchase a software company in a long time.

[I originally wrote this when it was at ~$60/share; however, I'm still excited about Rubrik today.]

Rubrik has experienced expanding margins and accelerating growth in recent quarters, and this is what I call a "potent cocktail."

All of the biggest winners of over the last few years have shared these two characteristics, i.e., expanding margins and accelerating growth. Companies such as Palantir (PLTR), CAVA (CAVA), Affirm (AFRM), Sea Ltd. (SE), and many others have experienced huge share price appreciation on the back of the potent cocktail of expanding margins and accelerating growth.

Additionally, Rubrik is doing this at a substantial scale.

First, subscription ARR [annual recurring revenue] reached $1.093 billion, growing 39% year-over-year, yet again a clear indication that we are winning the cyber resilience market. Net new subscription ARR reached over $90 million in the fourth quarter, which is a record for us and a standout for a cybersecurity company at our scale. Second, our subscription revenue was $244 million, growing 54% year-over-year. Third, our subscription NRR remained strong above 120%. Fourth, customers with $100k or more in subscription ARR reached 2,246 growing 29% year-over-year. Customers with $1 million or more in subscription ARR reached 162 growing 64% year-over-year. Finally, on profitability, we once again made material improvement in subscription ARR contribution margin up over 1,400 basis points year-over-year.

Bipul Sinha, CEO, Rubrik Q4 '24 Earnings Call

Rubrik's Accelerating Revenue Growth & Expanding Free Cash Flow Margins

In short, Rubrik is firing on all cylinders, and, at $16.5B in enterprise value, which is about 11.80x EV/2025 ARR, the company's risk/return setup is attractive today.

Let's now begin our review of Rubrik. We will use my systematic approach to understanding and ultimately buying stocks.

Here are the seven points of said systematic approach:

  1. Bull Thesis Simplified: Distilling the essence of the investment
  2. Bear Thesis Addressed: Distilling the risks and what could go wrong
  3. Foundational Investment Frameworks: Ensuring the business is qualitatively attractive
  4. Economic Moats: Identifying the key competitive advantages a business possesses
  5. Company In Charts: Assessing the most up-to-date key performance indicators
  6. Four Points Of Stock Value: Understanding why the stock trades where it does and where it should trade
  7. Valuation Model: Understanding what returns we could generate from an investment

Bull Thesis Simplified

  • Rubrik has carved out a data-protection and breach remediation niche in the multi-hundred billion dollar cybersecurity market, the size of which is set to grow for many years to come. Its stated mission is "Securing the world's data."
  • Its differentiation can be seen in a few pieces of key evidence: expanding margins alongside accelerating growth indicates that it's both taking market share (faster growth) and doing so with a product that commands pricing power (expanding margins). Additionally, it has been selected as Microsoft's (MSFT) Partner of the Year and is often bought adjacent to Microsoft software installments. If the king of software, i.e., Microsoft, needs your software and chooses to partner with you as opposed to replicate you, then you have very good, differentiated technology.
  • Through the lens of LASR's foundational investment frameworks, Rubrik fits well within the first and third today: it has built a unified platform, which has a variety of use cases seamlessly integrated into one platform, and it has done so via organic, multi-product innovation. Being a unified, multi-product platform provides Rubrik a number of benefits, such as greater embedding into its customers (more durable cash flows) and more avenues to go to market (faster, more efficient growth).

Where Rubrik Fits In The Cybersecurity Market

The Growth Of The Cybersecurity Market & Growth Of Ransomware (Threats)

Bear Thesis Addressed

I'll say that, given Rubrik's expanding margins and accelerating growth, at $1B+ ARR scale, it's frankly tough to be bearish. That said, here are key bear theses, and risks to consider:

  • Rubrik operates in the highly competitive software market. While software vendors can certainly develop differentiation in their products, their go-to-market motions, and their operational excellence, the number one variable that determines a software company's long-term success is its corporate culture. Can it continuously innovate such that it extends its runway for growth and further embeds itself in its customers' operations? Can it make acquisitions that are value-accretive? To be very sure, these are both extraordinarily challenging to accomplish at a giant scale. Only the strong survive, and only a small set of smaller software companies go on to be much larger software companies. Rubrik has, relative to the software giants in the market today, little history demonstrating its abilities in these key areas.
  • In a similar vein to point one, cybersecurity is rapidly, rapidly evolving, especially in the age of AI, and Rubrik must constantly evolve to meet the demands of this market, else it experiences slowing growth and difficulties winning business in the marketplace. We shall see if they have the ability to evolve over time.

Of course, growth investing is risky.

All investing, as, for instance, Intel has taught us in recent years, is risky.

That said, Rubrik is easily the most promising software business to go public since 2021; however, I still maintain that risk mitigation should be at the fore of our minds, and my primary tool for risk mitigation is diversification.

Foundational Investment Frameworks

Let's now review Rubrik through the lens of LASR's foundational investment frameworks.

  • Definition: I use my L.A. Stevens Research investment frameworks to select businesses.

Those frameworks include:

  1. Businesses that vertically integrate point solutions associated with an underwhelming consumer experience or product, the result of which is a vertically integrated product, or unified platform, that creates a differentiated consumer experience with likewise differentiated unit economics (which can make the platform a beneficiary of The Innovator's Dilemma, meaning that incumbents struggle to compete because that would mean altering the economics of their entire business model).
  2. Businesses that buy back shares routinely or are reaching a point of maturity where buybacks are just on the horizon
  3. Businesses that go multi-product, which is no small feat to be sure
  4. Businesses that can competently acquire, which is also no small feat

Let's start with the first:

Rubrik's Unified, Vertically Integrated Platform

Rubrik began as a data recovery and resilience platform, meaning that it offered software that remediated security breaches for enterprises.

However, today, it offers a holistic data security product, which it plans to augment and evolve over time.

Below, we can see graphical representations of this platform.

Rubrik's Unified Security Cloud Platform

Rubrik's Unified Security Cloud Platform

As we can see, Rubrik offers a host of products on its platform, all of which it has seamlessly integrated into "one pane of glass," i.e., one dashboard from which enterprises use its technology to secure their data, user identities, and address breaches of IT systems when they occur.

The primary value of fielding a platform of this kind is that it creates a substantial embedding moat: With Rubrik touching so many parts of an enterprise's tech stack, it is difficult to simply rip it out overnight. As Rubrik further expands the product set on its platform, i.e., the ways it serves enterprises, this embedding moat will grow over time, leading to even more durable cash flows.

Recall that we will review Rubrik through the four points of stock value (my proprietary valuation methodology) later on, so these ideas are important for populating those four points, the third of which is cash flow durability, and those four points will feed into our valuation model and the assumptions thereof.

Seamless cascading... (reference to an early idea I shared that these deep dives are thoughtfully crafted such that the information builds on itself until we have all the data necessary to formulate a valuation, which informs whether we buy, hold, or sell a stock... all the info of this review is seamlessly cascading towards the point of "buy, hold, or sell" at the end).

Of course, to build a unified, multi-product platform, a business must have the competence to develop or acquire new platform products and capabilities, which is very, very difficult for most businesses out there.

However, Rubrik appears to be among the hallowed few that is capable of doing so, which brings us to the third foundational framework.

Rubrik Has Gone Multi-Product

Before we begin this consideration of Rubrik's multi-product platform, I'd like to note that becoming multi-product is arguably the most valuable thing a company can do when it is young. It's so valuable, in fact, that I've written at length about the value of becoming multi-product, and I've shared these thoughts in what I titled, "The Seven Points Of Multi-Product Value." (Available for members of my Investing Group service.)

In the interest of brevity, I won't delineate all of those points for you today, but feel free to follow up with me in the comments.

The Seven Points Of Multi-Product Value granularly communicate why it's important for businesses of all kinds to develop multi-product platforms. With those points in mind, let's consider Rubrik's multi-product journey thus far.

In closing, I’m proud of our unwavering customer focus and relentless execution as we create a new vision for the cybersecurity industry based on cyber resilience. However, we are still early in Rubrik’s journey to realize the company’s full potential. In calendar 2025, we aim to accelerate our product innovation, generate groundbreaking ideas and lay foundation for continued future growth. I’m confident that what is ahead of us is even more important and exciting than what we have previously achieved.

Bipul Sinha, CEO, Rubrik Q4 '24 Earnings Call

On Rubrik's earnings calls, it devotes large portions of time to discussing its latest product innovations and directs analysts' attention to its latest products.

Rubrik began as just security breach remediation; however, today, it has evolved its platform such that it now offers data security, data access management, and data threat analysis.

Over time, it's likely that Rubrik further add products and services to its platform, whereby the platform becomes more valuable and the customers using the platform become more valuable to Rubrik and shareholders of Rubrik.

As the seven points of multi-product value state, two major points of value in being multi-product are 1) expanded customer value [CLTV] and 2) expanded avenues to go to market. To this end, on Rubrik's Q4 '24 earnings call, Kiran Choudary, CFO, shared,

We have a differentiated land-and-expand model, where we have multiple avenues to acquire new customers and expand the relationship with our customers after the initial contract.

[More avenues = faster growth; more avenues = more efficient sales teams who win deals because they show up with the right product at the right time to the right customer; more avenues = longer runway for growth]

We can expand through the growth of data in applications already secured by Rubrik through the expansion of our footprint of application secured and/or by the addition of more security functionality.

As a result, we continue to see a strong subscription net retention rate, which remained over 120% in the fourth quarter.

[More products = more upsells on customers, hence NRR of 120%. World-class NRR. Cannot be overstated.]

All vectors of expansion are healthy contributors to our NRR, highlighting the meaningful runway we have to more deeply penetrate our customer base. Adoption of additional security functionality is slightly over a third of our subscription net retention rate, up from approximately 29% in the year ago period.

Kiran Choudary, CFO, Rubrik Q4 '24 Earnings Call

We can see Rubrik's multi-product go to market motion in the chart below:

Each Box On Left Represents Distinct Products/Offerings To Go To Market With; The Right Represents Rubrik's Upsell Potential Via Its Multiple Products

And this multi-product evolution is very likely set to continue for many years to come.

If you look at our success today, as you said, Kash, our success today is a result of the work that we did four years or five years ago, where we built a platform that was futuristic, where we took the market risk and built a purpose-built platform for cyber resilience. And we are thinking about what do we do on Annapurna and all of these other new vectors of growth so that four years, five years from today, we still have a high growth curve. And that’s how we want to build a generational company. So a lot of opportunities.

As I said before, for us, we are not opportunity constrained. We need to ensure that we invest in the right market focus and continue to drive, again, a very profitable growth business.

Bipul Sinha, CEO, Rubrik Q4 '24 Earnings Call

In short, Rubrik has a multi-product culture in an industry where new product needs will proliferate for decades to come.

Fantastic.

The Fourth Framework: The Ability To Competently Acquire

Any larger company can acquire companies.

It happens all the time.

But what separates the generational companies from the mediocre, or outright bad, companies is the ability to not just acquire, but also to acquire in such a way that the folded-in business continues to flourish.

It's yet to be seen how well Rubrik can do this at scale; however, it's heartening to me to see that they've already begun acquiring new businesses, i.e., practice makes perfect, and they have intentions to do so again in the future.

Saket Kalia: So you’ve spent a lot of time with customers. How are you feeling about the TAM here for data protection? You talked about how customers need a more modern data protection tool as they move to the cloud and deliver GenAI. I think we see the share shift in numbers, but curious, if you feel the TAM here is expanding through that sort of cloud and GenAI transition as well.

Bipul Sinha: As you might recall, during IPO road show and in our subsequent quarterly updates, we have done a fundamental transformation of the traditional data protection market. We brought DSPM (data security posture management) and cyber recovery together to create a new platform to deliver cyber resilience. And this platform, Rubrik Security Cloud is a true data security platform. So by bringing this idea of data risk, data threat, and cyber recovery on a single platform, we massively expanded the TAM for our market. And as we had mentioned in our IPO prospectus, it’s over $50 billion market.

What is exciting is that as customers are taking a GenAI journey and preparing for GenAI as well as doing cloud transformation, the need for cyber resilience and data security is only increasing. As they are adopting more SaaS platforms, they need to secure that data, they need to ensure that the right data is available to the right user on the right platform at the right time for the right duration. And all of it is expanding the TAM for us.

So from where we sit, and as you can see this quarter results, we are not opportunity constrained. We are the only cybersecurity company that sits at the intersection of data security and AI. And it is on us going forward is, how do we prioritize our product development as well as inorganic tuck-ins to really accelerate our journey to the AI future.

Rubrik Q4 '24 Earnings Call

To close out this consideration of Rubrik through my foundational investment frameworks, the company fits perfectly in three of them.

Over time, I am sure it will fit within the second one as well, i.e., it will use excess free cash flow to buy back shares; however, today, it is still too young to do so.

Economic Moats

Let's now consider Rubrik through the lens of the four principal economic moats.

Remember, this is all feeding into the four points of stock value, and economic moats inform specifically the third point of stock value, i.e., cash flow durability. If a company has strong economic moats, then its cash flows are defensible: they are more durable the stronger its economic moats.

  1. Brand
  2. Network effects
  3. Economies of scale
  4. Switching costs/embedding moats

Brand

According to Rubrik's CEO, it is the only company that sits at the intersection of data security and AI.

While I think this could be hotly debated, I do think Rubrik has carved a niche in cybersecurity such that it stands alone in the specific value proposition it brings to customers.

That is, there's simply no other Rubrik in the public stock market today.

While CrowdStrike has S1, PANW, MSFT, CHKP, and others, and Okta has MSFT and CYBR, and Monday has HUBS and ASAN, Rubrik stands alone.

Over time, it very well could build a generational software brand for data security in the AI age.

Network Effects

Virtually all software companies benefit from shades of network effects. To be sure, not all network effects moats are created equal, e.g., Meta's Facebook network effects moat is far greater than Monday's network effects moat.

That said, for instance, as an enterprise loads more data into its Rubrik account, the value of the Rubrik platform grows for the enterprise.

More data on Rubrik = Rubrik can more effectively secure the enterprise holistically. Each incremental piece of data loaded onto the Rubrik platform makes the overall platform more valuable to the enterprise, which incentivizes the enterprise to do more business with Rubrik.

This lends to increased value of the enterprise to Rubrik, and to Rubrik's shareholders.

Economies Of Scale

Rubrik may benefit from economies of scale to some degree; however, I do not believe this is a significant moat for the company. It is not like Sea Ltd's Shopee requiring $50B worth of infrastructure investment over ten years to have the ability to sell paper plates at $1 instead of $5, which as an aside, feeds into network effects moat for Sea as more sellers show up to the platform because buyers can get $1 paper plates, which then attracts more buyers, and so on and so forth.

Switching Costs/Embedding Moat

This is certainly one of Rubrik's greatest moats. Imagine this scenario:

A Chief Information Security Officer [CISO] effectively bets his lucrative career, which required 30 years to achieve, on the Rubrik platform.

It's a big bet.

He then fields the platform in five different divisions in his company. All of those divisions train and standardize on the platform.

I just shared a huge outlay of risk and resources in choosing Rubrik.

To reverse course in a moment, ripping Rubrik out of all of the company's workflows and processes, removing data and transitioning the data to another platform, would be a fairly Herculean task involving a lot of resources and consternation, as well as a potential firing of the CISO.

Granted Rubrik does well in the first few years, and everything is copasetic, the enterprise bets even more on the Rubrik platform, placing more and more resources around and into the platform.

The stakes get higher and higher and the decision to reverse course harder and harder.

Hence, Rubrik has a solid embedding moat, making its cash flows utility-like in nature.

Let's now review some charts that detail the financials of Rubrik.

Rubrik In Charts

Below, we can see that Rubrik's growth at scale is simply sublime.

The company presently grows ARR at 39%, at $1B scale, which cannot be said for any other companies in the market, excepting CyberArk (CYBR).

We can also see that Rubrik's revenue largely derives from large customers with $100K of ARR. This implies that its cash flows are extremely durable, as it has de minimis exposure to SMBs (small to medium-sized businesses).

Lastly, we can see that Rubrik has a 120% net retention rate. This represents growth in spend from existing customers year over year.

120% is truly, truly world-class. This cannot be overstated. It puts Rubrik in a league with Snowflake (SNOW) and Palantir (PLTR), both of which trade at higher or farrr higher valuations.

Below, we can see that Rubrik has grown ARR (annual recurring revenue) consistently over the years, and, in fact, actually accelerated ARR growth in its recent quarter.

Expanding margins + accelerating growth = a potent cocktail for share price appreciation.

Rubrik ARR Growth

Below, the above data is illustrated again:

Rubrik Growth By Cohort (Note Expansion Of Spend Over Time) & Customers With $100K ARR Count

Turning to 2025 guidance, below we can see that Rubrik guided for 24% ARR growth, to $1.35B, and 30% revenue growth.

Of great note, Rubrik beat estimates by an eye-watering $25M(!) in Q4 '24.

It's likely that ARR growth actually comes in well above 30% in 2025, and management alluded to the idea that 24% was conservative on their most recent earnings call.

Rubrik Guidance

A few things to call out to give context on the guidance, one we’ve talked about earlier as well and I mentioned that in my script we got about two points of benefit to our ARR growth in fiscal 2025 from the remaining part of the maintenance to subscription transition. That impact on net new ARR is higher than the ARR base. I’ll also say that we are early in the year we are giving our first annual guide as a public company.

I want to be really thoughtful. As Bipul has talked about, we see strong demand, continued demand for cyber resilience but also want to put numbers we feel really confident about and look forward to finishing the year strong.

Kiran Choudary, CFO, Rubrik Q4 '24 Earnings Call

Alright, so we've walked through the qualitative and quantitative elements of the Rubrik business. With all of this information in mind, we can now populate the four points of stock value with greater precision.

Four Points Of Stock Value

  1. The amount of free cash flow per share a business generates (we need to understand margins and share count trends to fully nail this down). Rubrik recently did 29% free cash flow margins, beneath 80% gross margins. The economic nature of its software platform and competitive positioning suggest that 35% free cash flow margins long term will be more than achievable. Rubrik will dilute pretty significantly in the year ahead, growing shares from 192M to 198M; however, with such big free cash flow margins already, dilution should slow materially in the years ahead.
  2. The growth of that free cash flow per share (we need to understand TAM). The cyber resilience TAM might only be $5B; however, Rubrik's evolution into a multi-product platform whose mission is "securing the world's data" has expanded this TAM dramatically. Rubrik states that its current TAM is $53B. Maybe that's true, maybe not. What's more important is that the company has demonstrated the ability to go multi-product, thereby consistently expanding its TAM. It's also clearly messaged that it has a large new product opportunity set in front of it on which it plans to capitalize in the years ahead.
  3. The durability of that free cash flow per share (we need to understand economic moats and competitive advantages that create defensible and durable cash flows). We spent a great deal of time focusing on this element of the thesis. Rubrik's embedding moat is profound as a mission-critical cybersecurity platform in a world where cybersecurity threats are growing by the day, or perhaps by the moment as AIs do not rest.
  4. Our next best alternative (in the stock market, this is the yield on the 10 year and 2 year treasury bonds. If you can get a guaranteed 30% annual yield elsewhere, you should stop reading and go take advantage of that. But the 10 year is only 4.3% today, so, if that's your next best alternative, as it is mine, then stick with me). Inflation is dead. The trade war is being walked back and deals are being signed. Yields should come down. (I'm staying positive through what is ultimately a totally unpredictable macro-environment).

Regarding the second point, I found it noteworthy that Rubrik's revenues are well-diversified. It currently generates a large portion of its revenue from international markets.

Turning to the geographic mix of revenue: revenue from the Americas grew 48% to $185 million; revenue from outside the Americas grew 47% to $73 million.

Kiran Choudary, CFO, Rubrik Q4 '24 Earnings Call

Software is so attractive because it can be sold into global markets, which are vast and growing by the day.

Let's now perform our valuation modeling.

Valuation Model

My assumptions are as follows:

Assumptions:

TTM Revenue [A]

 $1B

Potential Free Cash Flow Margin [B]

35%

Total diluted shares outstanding [C]

198M

Free cash flow per share [ D = (A * B) / C ]

$1.77

Free cash flow per share growth rate

20%

Terminal growth rate

3%

Years of elevated growth

10

Total years to stimulate

100

Discount Rate (Our "Next Best Alternative")

9.8%

And here are the results:

Notably, I used a conservative 20% annualized growth rate.

Considering Rubrik is growing at 47% currently and guided for 30% growth this year, which it will likely beat, an average annualized growth rate of 20% should be very easily achieved.

Turning to total projected returns...

Total Projected Returns

I used a 35x exit multiple for the 10 year modeling period because I believe Rubrik will continue to grow at healthy rates long after the 10 years have concluded.

And, as we can see, we're being offered about 15% annualized returns.

Rubrik is the first new software company I'm excited about in a very long time.

We're getting great leadership, quality product, rapid growth, attractive valuation, healthy balance sheet, free cash flow generation, strong moats and market positioning, and a multi-product corporate culture... It's awesome.

In closing, I’m proud of our unwavering customer focus and relentless execution as we create a new vision for the cybersecurity industry based on cyber resilience. However, we are still early in Rubrik’s journey to realize the company’s full potential. In calendar 2025, we aim to accelerate our product innovation, generate groundbreaking ideas and lay foundation for continued future growth. I’m confident that what is ahead of us is even more important and exciting than what we have previously achieved.

Bipul Sinha, CEO, Rubrik Q4 '24 Earnings Call

In short, I like Rubrik at $86/share.

Please let me know if you have any questions, comments, and concerns.

data.title: First Majestic Silver: Printing Paper Even Faster Than The Federal Reserve
data.description:
Jerritt Canyon Acquisition

In March 2021, First Majestic Silver (AG) announced the acquisition of the Jerritt Canyon Mine in Nevada. In return, Sprott Mining received $470 million worth of First Majestic shares in addition to 5.0 million warrants with an exercise price of $20.00 per share and a term of three years. Concurrent with the transaction, Eric Sprott also injected $30 million in the company through a private placement at a price of $17.59 per share.

For the full year of 2020, Jerritt Canyon reported a production volume of 112,749 ounces of gold at a cash cost of US$1,289 per ounce. After having identified various opportunities to improve volumes and operating costs, First Majestic's management thought that Jerritt Canyon would add a valuable gold weighting to the company's portfolio, in addition to diversifying away from Mexico. After the transaction, Eric Sprott, along with some of his affiliated companies, owned ~13.1% of First Majestic's shares outstanding.

In March 2023, First Majestic announced the suspension of all mining activities at Jerritt Canyon, effective immediately. Mining rates had been below the 3,000 tonnes per day initially expected by management, and along with other operational issues, production costs ballooned. To put things in perspective, Jerritt Canyon represented approximately 21% of AG's revenues in 2022. We are now more than two years into the 'temporary suspension', and there is still no clear plan to restart production.

In 2024, First Majestic completed limited work on the property, while an 18,000-meter drilling program is planned for 2025. What was bought as a cash-generating asset is now a cash-consuming asset. The price tag? 26.7 million shares. At $5.50 per share, the paper issued for the Jerritt Canyon acquisition is now worth ~$150 million. Yes, there is a lot of infrastructure, but that's pretty much it for now. One thing is also clear: rust is working day and night and never stops. It is particularly true for idle equipments already decades into their economic lives. After all, Jerritt Canyon started production in 1981, almost 45 years ago.

To buy the very asset that an astute mining investor like Eric Sprott is selling, you need some conviction. What about the risk of asymmetric information between the buyer and the seller? In general terms, the seller of a house may always know a little bit more than the buyer. If it looks like a bargain, why is it so? I have been a bargain chaser in my younger years, and with today's perspective, I can see that I have been the fool more than a few times. My guess is that Eric Sprott had a solid understanding of the asset while also being aware that First Majestic's paper may be overvalued.

In fact, March 2021 was the perfect time for First Majestic to issue a paper. In hindsight, it is obvious that the problem with the Jerritt Canyon transaction was not the 'when', as the timing was pretty much perfect. The problem surrounds the what, or the quality of the asset bought with the freshly issued paper. Issuing paper at the right time is only half of the equation. Over the long term, what truly matters is how the capital raised is used. It is precisely on this topic that the financial interests of minority shareholders may differ from those of management. Of course, it is not unique to First Majestic, as any publicly listed company is subject to similar agency risks. However, they may be more pronounced in capital-intensive industries like mining.

In a book published in March 2021, Mark Carney, the new Prime Minister of Canada, highlighted the limited rights of shareholders that can lead to agency problems and ultimately, to the erosion of shareholder value. When you hold the metal, you don't have to deal with third parties and the associated risks. Some will even say that if you cannot touch the metal, you do not own it. In this case, if some clearinghouse deep inside the financial system becomes insolvent, there is no risk of bail-in, similar to what is discussed in the book 'The Great Taking' published by David Rogers Webb in the fall of 2023.

The second false premise is that shareholders are owners of a corporation rather than its residual claimants. If, as we have seen, shareholders are not owners in the conventional sense, the limits to shareholder primacy become apparent. Lynn Sout, professor at Cornell Law School, pointed out that what shareholders own are the shares themselves, which are a type of contract that gives them specific and limited rights. Values: Building a Better World for All; Mark Carney

How many assets have no counterparty risks? How much is this characteristic worth in a world where the risks of systemic failure are rising? It is fair to say that we are still early in the game regarding counterparty risks. At least for now, it has not really entered mass consciousness for quite a long time. Except for a brief period in March 2020, an entire generation of investment advisors and other finance professionals has never considered it.

If history rhymes, this topic could once again become front and center sometime down the road. What if understanding how clearinghouses work has never been so important? What if risks cannot be eliminated, but only transferred? What happens when all those risks are transferred and hidden in the same place?

Regulators must equip themselves with tools such as bail-in bonds to deal quickly with a failed clearing house for stocks, bonds or derivatives without having to call on taxpayers for cash, the G20's risk watchdog said on Thursday. Regulators told to be ready to handle failed clearing houses; Reuters

Talking about some of those tail risks may sound extreme, but I believe they become important variables to consider when deciding between investing in physical silver, paper silver, miners, or derivative contracts. Each vehicle has a different risk and reward proposition, and it is up to each of us to choose the one that best fits our financial goals. I don't think there is a universal answer, and it is precisely why there is demand for all of them.

We only have to go back a few decades in the United States to find an era when physical gold ownership was illegal. In August 1934, via Executive Order 6814, President Franklin D. Roosevelt also implemented the seizure of all silver situated in the continental United States. This shows that some tail risks around liquidity and transaction costs are present even with physical metals. You cannot eliminate risks completely, but you can choose the ones that won't stop you from sleeping comfortably at night.

People who were simply protecting themselves and their families from the actions of the Federal Reserve System were accused of hoarding gold, and literally criminalized if they persisted in doing so. I asked my father why people had turned in their gold. He said that if you did not you were a criminal, but further, that there was nothing you would be able to do with it because you could not legally transport or sell it. So, essentially, the use and value of gold had been confiscated. This was certainly the case because it remained illegal for a U.S. person to own gold for more than forty years! The Great Taking; David Rogers Webb

For First Majestic shareholders, Jerritt Canyon is an old story, but it highlights some of the idiosyncratic risks associated with miners. Over the last three years, silver prices have increased by about 50% while First Majestic's share price has declined by roughly 30%. The picture is even more depressing when looking at gold's relative performance, as its price has increased by almost 75% over the same time frame. Of note, gold accounted for 33% of AG's revenues in the first quarter of 2025, a sharp decline versus the 57% reported in the same quarter last year. The drop in the company's gold weighting is mostly due to the inclusion of the Cerro Los Gatos silver mine in the most recent financial statements.

Investors wanting to express their bullish view on spot silver or precious metals in general by buying shares of First Majestic have failed miserably over the last few years. In my view, the acquisition of Jerrit Canyon is one of the main reasons. It is also a reminder that with the ownership of miners, there are important risks surrounding capital allocation. The potential to compound capital is present, but the risks of permanent capital losses are also real. That's only one lesson behind the Jerritt Canyon debacle.

Cerro Los Gatos Acquisition

In September 2024, First Majestic announced a definitive merger agreement with Gatos Silver. The key asset underpinning the $970 million transaction was a 70% ownership interest in the Los Gatos Joint Venture, which in turn owns the producing Cerro Los Gatos underground silver mine in Chihuahua, Mexico. Of note, the remaining 30% ownership interest is owned by Dowa Metals & Mining, a subsidiary of Dowa Holdings, a Japanese conglomerate.

After the transaction closure in January 2025 and the issuance of 177 million shares, former Gatos shareholders owned approximately 38% of AG's fully diluted shares outstanding. If we include the 26.7 million shares issued for the Jerritt Canyon transaction discussed earlier, we are talking about ~205 million shares or ~40% of the 501.5 million fully diluted shares outstanding as of the end of March 2025.

If we also take into consideration the various financings and equity awards to the management and board of directors, First Majestic's shares outstanding have almost tripled since the end of 2016. In itself, a rapidly increasing number of shares outstanding is a neutral fact, and with judicious capital allocation, it may even be a good thing.

First Majestic ended 2016 with a production volume of 18.7 million silver-equivalent ounces compared to a 2025 production guidance of 29.5 million silver-equivalent ounces at the midpoint. While the number of shares outstanding increased by almost 300%, production volumes increased by only ~60%. That's my core issue with investing my hard-earned capital in First Majestic's shares.

According to the 2025 World Silver Survey, Cerro Los Gatos is the 14th largest silver-producing mine in the world today. As illustrated below, the ranking is dominated by polymetallic mines, including the well-known Peñasquito Mine owned by Newmont (NEM) or the Juanicipio Mine owned by Fresnillo (OTCPK:FNLPF) and MAG Silver (MAG).

Primary silver mines account for less than 30% of global mine production, meaning that approximately 70% of primary silver production comes as a by-product of copper, lead, zinc and gold mines. This dynamic creates an inelastic supply function, as silver prices do not necessarily trigger a commensurate response in supply. Silver: A Strategic Industrial And Monetary Metal In Structural Deficit

Cerro Los Gatos is also a polymetallic mine, with around 30% of its silver-equivalent ounces coming from gold, lead, and zinc. Considering First Majestic's corporate strategy, I understand the attractiveness of Cerro Los Gatos. Large primary silver mines are relatively rare; they do not come on the market very often. First Majestic expects annual free cash flows of ~$70 million from Cerro Los Gatos. Relative to the $970 million purchase price, we are then talking about a free cash flow yield around 7.5%.

During an interview with Kitco News, Keith Neumeyer made it clear that the transaction with Gatos Silver came after 'a very competitive process'. BofA Securities acted as the exclusive financial advisor to Gatos Silver during the transaction, and I have no doubt that a group of investment bankers worked relentlessly to market the deal as much as they could. Moreover, the key capital provider behind Gatos Silver was the Electrum Group, led by the renowned resource investor Thomas Kaplan. It would have been very hard for the Electrum Group to dispose of their 32% stake in Gatos Silver, considering the stock's low liquidity. For Thomas Kaplan and his team, the deal with First Majestic provided some exit liquidity, and we should not be surprised that Electrum sold 3.0 million shares in March 2025.

The Electrum Group, Thomas Kaplan, and BofA Securities are not in the business of leaving money on the table. Earlier in this article, I pretty much said the same thing about Eric Sprott and the Jerritt Canyon transaction. In contrast, approximately 44% of First Majestic's shares outstanding are owned by retail investors. In other words, a good chunk of the paper issued by First Majestic over the years ended up in the hands of retail investors. In the end, the capital generated from the sale of stocks ultimately ended up in the hands of Thomas Kaplan and Eric Sprott. The story of Jerritt Canyon is a disaster, and the price paid for Cerro Los Gatos was dear, although I fully acknowledge the high quality of the asset. In this whole story, retail investors have once again been the net losers.

Conclusion

Investors in precious metals generally recognized the importance of hard currencies and of protecting oneself against monetary debasement. They often talk about the massive creation and injection of liquidity in the financial system in times of crisis, and on that topic, the financial crisis of 2008 and the COVID-19 pandemic are great examples. In both instances, the U.S. national debt and its associated liquidity pushed financial markets to record highs.

If we view First Majestic's stock as a currency, we can see a debasement that is much faster than anything seen with Western central banks. While the company issued paper to purchase assets, the added production volumes and cash flows were never enough to compensate. If we look over the last few years, we can see that there has been a leak in this entire process. A leak that has been mostly paid by minority shareholders.

According to the 2025 management information circular, Keith Neumeyer owns approximately 4.3 million shares, and the total increases to 6.4 million shares if stock-options, RSUs, and PSUs are included. How much of this ~$40 million stake was earned through stock options and grants, and how much of it was earned through market purchases with hard dollars? Minority shareholders depend entirely on rising stock prices to make money, but it is quite different for management teams with juicy compensation packages. This is precisely where the financial interests of the two groups may differ.

My 'Hold' rating on First Majestic reflects my optimistic views on precious metals over the long term. In a world in which the safety of the U.S. Treasury market is increasingly questioned, it is in precious metals that I see the safety. The unsustainable fiscal position of the United States with its record fiscal deficit are simply the other side of the same coin.

Unfortunately for minority shareholders, the debasement of First Majestic's stock has been pronounced over the last few years. Yes, the stock can certainly provide leverage to silver prices in the short run, but I doubt it is the ideal vehicle for a buy-and-hold strategy. Don't get me wrong, the shares are highly liquid, and it is a great trading vehicle, but it is simply not what I am looking for. So, no, thank you!

data.title: John Deere: Autonomous Tractors And Precision Agriculture In Action
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from The Nightview Fund Q1 2025 Letter.

In February, our team took a trip—arguably ill-timed when it was -10 degrees)—to Moline, Illinois – home of John Deere (NYSE:DE) – to get a close look at how autonomy and AI are revolutionizing agriculture.

John Deere has been aggressively integrating advanced technology into farm equipment, effectively turning tractors and combines into high-tech robots of the field.

We spent time with Deere’s CTO and CFO, saw demos of autonomous tractors, and spoke to former execs and customers using Deere’s precision ag tools.

The result was a deeper appreciation for how automation is boosting yield, cutting costs, and addressing the acute labor challenges in farming.

Here are the key insights and data points from that field visit.

Autonomous Tractors: From Concept to Reality

A highlight of our visit was learning about Deere’s latest autonomous tractor, a retrofitted 8R series tractor equipped with a suite of cameras and sensors.

This is the same system Deere first unveiled at CES 2022. It uses six pairs of stereo cameras for 360-degree vision and advanced AI models to navigate fields without a driver. The machine is hooked up to a chisel plow, performing tillage on a test field – completely on its own.

It carefully stayed in the bounds of the field, detected and stopped for obstructions (we saw it identify a stray log lying in the path), and sent real-time video and alerts to the farmer’s phone. We were struck by how “normal” the tractor looks – aside from the camera pods, there’s no flashy antenna or anything; the brain is largely invisible.

Deere is not stopping at the 8R row-crop tractor. At CES 2025 (this January), the company announced a second-generation autonomy platform and plans to extend it to more types of equipment: smaller tractors for orchards and vineyards, and even construction machines like articulated dump trucks for quarries. The CTO, Jahmy Hindman, whom we met briefly, emphasized that we are in early innings for development.

Addressing Labor Shortage & Efficiency

Agriculture is facing a well-documented labor crunch. The average U.S. farmer is nearly 60 years old, and younger people are less inclined to do strenuous farm work​. By one estimate, in 2024 the U.S. AG industry had 2.4 million fewer workers than needed​, and this gap is only growing.

We heard anecdotes from farmers about advertising tractor operator jobs with little uptake. Autonomous tractors are arriving just in time to fill this gap. This highlights a key advantage: 24/7 operation. An autonomous tractor doesn’t need to rest. It can prepare fields overnight while the farmer sleeps.

Precision Agriculture: Data-Driven Yield Improvements

Autonomy in driving is one aspect; precision agriculture (using data and AI to optimize farming) is another area where Deere is leading, and the two go hand in hand. We delved into Deere’s See & Spray™ technology, which is an AI-driven system that identifies weeds in the field and targets them with herbicide, instead of blanket spraying the whole field.

The results are astounding: in the 2024 growing season, farmers using See & Spray reported on average 59% less herbicide use on their corn, soybean, and cotton fields​. In total, Deere calculated about 8 million gallons of herbicide mix saved across 1+ million acres using this tech in 2024​.

data.title: Natera: The Clues In Q1 Earnings
data.description:
Introduction

Natera (NASDAQ:NTRA) is a genetic testing company, with focuses on oncology, women's health, and organ health. Their signature product, Signatera, can help predict the efficacy of many forms of cancer treatment. Natera has also been heavily investing in AI, with prominent product launches in this area going back as far as 2021. Legendary fund manager Stanley Druckenmiller has Natera as his current number one holding.

However, Natera is currently unprofitable and is also relatively expensive—an 11 P/S ratio is high compared to the average of 5.5 for Biotech or 4.8 for Life Science Tools—so in order to justify an investment, we will need to find clues that Natera's trajectory is on track. As I have mentioned in other articles, I believe that in order for a growth stock to outperform, it must not only keep growing, but grow faster than expectations.

With that in mind, I want to take some time today to focus on insights from Natera's recent Q1 earnings, and see what insights we can glean in terms of modeling out future outperformance.

Natera Smashes Expectations

Natera's Q1 results were a home run, with revenue of $501.8M (+36.5% Y/Y) beating by $55.13M, and GAAP EPS of -$0.50 beating by $0.12. Despite these results, as well as a guidance raise, Natera's stock fell by 6.5% the next day. This echoed the pattern of peer GeneDx (WGS), which also beat expectations but saw the stock fall anyway. There's been pressure on the sentiment for healthcare stocks recently, so I suppose this is to be expected.

Still, it's hard to overstate how good Q1 was—especially considering that other companies have mentioned that Q1 is usually the toughest for volume for any lab. Natera's management noted that:

We had an outstanding volume quarter across the board with 855,000 units processed in the quarter. This included a big step-up in women's health volumes over Q4 of last year and a record volume quarter for Signatera. Signatera clinical volumes grew 52% year-on-year and increased by roughly 16,500 units compared to Q4, which is our best sequential unit quarter yet for Signatera.

Additionally, gross margins continued to rise, hitting 63%:

On the call, management also noted that "Signatera ASPs moved above $1,100 in the quarter," which is important because they later noted that "Guidelines in the United States and Japan for Signatera have the potential to move ASPs above $2,000 per test."

In other words, not only is Signatera's volume still growing at breakneck speed, and management also believes that the value per test could nearly double. Combining both of these led management to conclude that eventually, Signatera could be a $5 billion dollar a year product. Natera's current annual revenue is $1.8 billion, and that's for all product lines, not just Signatera, so this would represent a substantial increase.

No Weather Excuses—Unlike GeneDx

During the GeneDx call, management blamed poor weather in Q1, such as the LA fires, for a lighter than usual testing volume. I believe management was aware of this, and gave them a subtle callout—note the reference to other players in the space:

I mean certainly, weather was a huge challenge in the quarter, particularly with respect to the wildfires in Southern California, that's a very important area of the country for us, very important territory. You'd be hard pressed to see it in the volumes however.... They need their carrier screening testing, they need their Signatera test, and they find a way to get back in relatively short order back to the physician's office and get that testing done. I think that concept has massive implications for how efficient the commercialization model is for Natera relative to a lot of players even within the molecular diagnostics space...

I believe this demonstrates the superiority of Natera's far more established business model vs GeneDx. This is to be expected, though, since GeneDx is priced at roughly half the price to sales ratio of Natera.

True-Ups and Guidance

Natera guided for roughly $2 billion of revenue in 2025 at the midpoint. However, this guide excludes the impact of "true-ups," which are revenue belatedly received from tests conducted in prior quarters. Natera received $34 million in true-ups this quarter, but guided for zero for the rest of the year:

But for the remainder of the year, just presume zero, and that goes for the gross margin guide as well. If you ask me, will we have some true-ups. Yes, we will. They're just hard to predict and I think they make it harder for you guys to model it, so we'll leave it out.

In other words, true-ups will continue to roll in. Analysts have not accounted for this at all, with the current consensus analyst estimate sitting at exactly $2 billion for 2025 as of this writing. This will allow Natera to get the benefit of continuing to beat expectations, even if the amount of true-ups is relatively small.

Natera's overall guidance is for 26% "pro forma" growth, which discounts the impact of the large amount of true-ups in 2024. But is that a realistic estimate, or is Natera deliberately sandbagging their own guidance?

A Look Back at 2024's Q1 Guidance

To judge exactly how conservative Natera is being, let's return to Q1 2024. At that time, Natera guided for $1.435 billion for 2024. Natera ended up turning in $1.7 billion, over 18% above the guide. Natera has outperformed guidance in prior years as well, guiding for $0.995 billion in Q1 of 2023, and beating that estimate too. This trend bodes well for the assumption that Natera's guidance is deliberately over-conservative.

Valuation and Options

As of this writing, Natera's stock still sits below the price it was before their report, which seems like an opportunity. I still view the aforementioned high valuation as the primary negative. Natera is expensive both relative to peers and relative to its own historical metrics:

If you cannot stomach owning this stock at such a high price, you can attempt to reduce your cost by selling put options at a lower strike instead of owning it outright.

Risks

Competition is always a risk in a field like this one. However, management believes they are in a strong position, with doctors raising issues with competitors:

And they're also raising concerns about specificity problems with some of the other genome based laboratories.... They're raising concerns about extreme analytical claims not necessarily translating into clinical performance...

There are other risks as well, such as potential complications to the healthcare reimbursement sector thanks to GOP efforts to cut Medicaid and change reimbursement rules, as well as poor sentiment around healthcare stocks in general thanks to the FDA and HHS.

The Bottom Line

Natera turned in another amazing quarter, along with a guidance raise and a long-term vision for spectacular growth. The company's track record and hints from the earnings call indicate that it is poised to beat expectations. The primary issue is valuation. Still, expensive stocks can overperform, check out Costco (COST) if you doubt this. I tentatively rate Natera a Buy, especially if you can manage to snag some shares on a dip.

data.title: Tesla: Cybercab, Humanoid Robotics And More
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from The Nightview Fund Q1 2025 Letter.

Let’s take a step back.

Investor apprehension often peaks when disruptive visions collide with hard engineering reality – and few visions have been as bold, or as debated, as Tesla’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) quest for fully autonomous vehicles. In June, however, Tesla’s long-promised autonomy strategy is reaching a critical milestone. Tesla’s team is preparing to launch an autonomous ride-hailing service in Austin, Texas, featuring a dedicated robotaxi vehicle dubbed the “Cybercab.” This is not a distant white paper scenario; it’s slated to begin June 2025 as an operational pilot​.

And while this is only the first limited step, for the first time, Tesla vehicles will be rolling through city streets with no human driver in the front seat – a moment that we believe could mark Tesla’s transition from testing self-driving technology to deploying it commercially.

FSD Progress and Tesla’s Data Advantage:

Tesla’s Full Self-Driving (FSD) platform has evolved rapidly over the past year. Internally, Tesla reports that its FSD Beta software (installed in hundreds of thousands of customer-owned cars) is achieving dramatically higher “miles between interventions” – a key metric indicating how far a car can go on autopilot before a human must take over.

While competitors like Waymo have made headlines operating completely driverless cars in limited urban geofences (an important proof of concept), Tesla has been amassing an unparalleled volume of real-world driving data. According to Alphabet, Waymo’s commercial robotaxi fleet drives about 200,000 miles per day autonomously in its service areas, as of February 2025. Tesla’s FSD, by contrast, is already logging roughly 10 million miles per day in autonomous or assisted mode across a far broader range of roads and conditions​.

In other words, even before launching its robotaxi service, Tesla enjoys about a 50x data advantage in real-world driving experience over Waymo​.

This massive scale of driving data – covering myriad edge cases, weather, and road types – acts as a flywheel to improve Tesla’s AI driving models. Every mile provides training feedback.

We continue to believe that data itself is the new gold mine in AI. The rush for novel and useful information can be seen throughout. Encyclopedia Britannica, a company older than the United States has seen a rush towards its data set propelling its business once derided as an antiquity. While this one case — we believe that the more novel and unique (see: autonomous driver edge cases) the more valuable.

Our view is that Tesla is effectively tackling autonomy with the largest real-world driving dataset on Earth, which we believe will only enable more rapid iteration and improvement once true driverless operations begin.

Indeed, Musk has argued that Tesla’s vision-only approach (using cameras without lidar) can work because neural nets trained on billions of miles will eventually handle edge cases as well as or better than dedicated sensors. The upcoming Austin pilot will be the proof point to watch.

It’s worth noting that Tesla’s strategy has shifted subtly but significantly. For years, Musk promised that existing Teslas would receive an over-the-air update and effectively become robotaxis overnight – a “flip the switch” approach. In practice, reaching full Level 4/5 autonomy has been harder than expected, and Tesla is now complementing its software-first strategy with a controlled rollout using a dedicated fleet (much like Waymo and Cruise).

The difficulty of achieving an autonomous, portable, ubiquitous and affordable system has the potential to increase the value of this technology. If it was easy everyone would and could be doing it.

The Cybercab unveiled in late 2024—which we attended—is a purpose-built robo-taxi: a compact two-seater electric vehicle optimized for autonomy and ride-hailing​. It boasts an ultra-streamlined design with only 80 structural parts (versus 200+ in a Model Y), enabling low-cost mass production – reportedly under $30,000 per unit​, according to Tesla in October 2024.

By deploying Cybercabs in one city with geofencing and remote supervision, Tesla can validate full self-driving in a relatively safe setting before scaling up. This is a departure from relying solely on customers to beta-test on public roads; it indicates a maturation in Tesla’s approach to match the caution of competitors. In June, if Cybercabs indeed hit the streets of Austin without drivers, it will be a major milestone – Tesla’s first commercial robotaxi service after years of incremental FSD updates​.

Tesla’s Data and AI Lead:

Why do we believe Tesla is at the cusp of an autonomy breakthrough when many have tried and failed?

The simple answer is data.

This is a classic “tortoise and the hare” story. Waymo sprinted ahead early, but we believe it may be stuck at a local maximum—limited by its reliance on expensive hardware and tightly geofenced maps. Tesla, in contrast, chose a slower but more scalable path. By prioritizing low-cost cameras and mass deployment, it’s building a general solution with far greater long-term potential.

While Waymo uses lidar and HD maps to guide cars in limited areas, Tesla relies on a vision-based system trained by real-world driving data. As of early 2025, more than 4 million Teslas with Autopilot/FSD hardware are on the road, constantly collecting footage and telemetry. No other company—even all AV firms combined—comes close to that scale. As one former Lyft autonomy exec told us in a recent In Practise interview:

“Data is crucial, especially now when we look at the big models, even in autonomous driving. Consider the foundational models from OpenAI, Google, and others. They have utilized almost all available data from the Internet or that they can purchase. However, they still struggle with tasks involving street scenes.” Source: This interview is featured on the In Practise Platform, a primary research service .(Source: Error | In Practise)

All In Practise interviews are for informational purposes only and should not be relied upon as a basis for investment decisions. In Practise is an independent publisher and all opinions expressed by guests are solely their own opinions and do not reflect the opinion of In Practise or Nightview.

In other words, Tesla’s huge fleet effectively crowdsources the solution to self-driving by learning from millions of human decisions and mistakes on the road. This data advantage does not guarantee success (it must still be translated into robust driving policy by AI), but it creates a high barrier to entry. If Tesla’s FSD AI can reach human-level reliability, we believe it can immediately be deployed to a fleet orders of magnitude larger than any competitor’s​.

That scaling potential is one reason we remain bullish on Tesla’s approach.

Current Progress – Are we there yet?

While in the immediate term we approach some of Tesla’s autonomy claims with measured optimism. FSD (Supervised) v13.2.8 for Hardware 4 (HW4) has steadily improved. And we believe the rate of improvement validates the company’s approach.

Users report the latest builds handle complex urban maneuvers much more confidently than a year ago – unprotected left turns, roundabouts, and even some corner-case interactions like debris on the road.

While Musk’s timelines are famously optimistic, by Q1 2025 Tesla indeed felt confident enough to schedule the unsupervised Cybercab launch. Notably, Tesla is adding a layer of remote human oversight for safety – they have hired teleoperators who can monitor the robotaxis and intervene via remote control if a vehicle gets confused. (Source.)

This “unsupervised but not unmonitored” approach is prudent, in our view. It acknowledges that true general AI-level driving may still falter occasionally, but those edge cases can be managed with a human backstop.

In essence, Tesla is doing what Cruise and Waymo do (remote fleet supervision), but with a far larger pool of situations to learn from. If the Austin pilot goes well – i.e., minimal disengagements and no serious incidents – it could unlock a path to scale this service to other cities and eventually eliminate the remote monitor step.

Total Addressable Market (TAM) for Autonomous Ride-Hailing:

The economic opportunity behind Tesla’s robotaxi push is enormous. By turning cars into software-driven revenue generators, Tesla aims to transform the business model of transportation. Instead of selling a car once, a robotaxi can potentially earn tens of thousands of dollars per year by providing transportation as a service.

Importantly, the total addressable market in terms of consumer spend on global transportation is even larger – our calculations suggest trillions of dollars of economic redistribution could be credited to a shift towards autonomy—which we wrote about here in Q3 2024.

This is why Tesla – and we as investors – see autonomy as a multi-decade value driver. What could this market be worth to Tesla specifically? In a recent earnings call, Elon Musk has boldly predicted that Tesla’s robotaxi network, combined with its humanoid robots (more on Optimus later), could make Tesla the most valuable company in the world. In a recent earnings call.

And while our own estimates are more conservative, but still staggering: even a few percent share of global miles shifting to Tesla robotaxis in the late 2020s could yield billions in incremental operating profit.

Cybercab Launch – What to Watch:

In June, Tesla’s Austin pilot will begin with a limited service area and a fleet of company-owned vehicles (initially, possibly modified Model Ys and early Cybercab prototypes). As the service scales commercially, we expect the long-run cost curve to trend downward as utilization increases and human drivers (and their labor cost) are removed.

Tesla has a huge margin opportunity here: thanks to EVs’ lower operating costs (about one-third the cost of gasoline vehicles) and expected high utilization, Tesla could undercut competitors while still earning a healthy “take rate” on each trip​.

The Cybercab rollout will also test Tesla’s safety and public trust. The company claims that even in beta form, FSD-equipped cars have accident rates far lower than average.

In summary, Tesla’s FSD platform is entering a prove-it period.

The groundwork – in data, AI, and vehicle design – has been laid to enable a true driverless service. Investors often ask us, “When will autonomy really happen at scale?” Our answer is that the pivot is happening now.

Like the rollout of the internet or smartphones, adoption may seem gradual and then sudden. We expect Tesla to start small in Austin, gather feedback, then rapidly iterate its software. If all goes well, by 2026 Tesla could be operating autonomous fleets in multiple U.S. cities.

We believe the Total Addressable Market (TAM) is in the trillions, the competitive moat is significant (data, vertical integration, unit economics), and the consumer value proposition (cheaper, safer transport) is extremely compelling. This is why we remain long-term believers in Tesla and view 2025 as potentially the year Tesla “cracks the code” on commercial self-driving.

In our view, it’s the dawn of the Cybercab era – and potentially the start of a transformation in how humans move around.

Autonomy’s Economic Transformation: Low-Cost Transport and New Use Cases

Stepping back from Tesla specifically, let’s consider the broader implications of ubiquitous autonomy.

We often talk internally about the deflationary effect of technology – how advances drive down costs and unlock new demand. Autonomous vehicles ('AVS') are a prime example. By automating the driver, we are poised to radically reduce the cost per mile of transportation, perhaps to levels never seen in modern times. This is not just about Uber fares getting cheaper; it’s about fundamentally changing what is possible in logistics, urban planning, and consumer behavior.

Cost-Per-Mile Revolution

Today, owning and operating a personal car costs around $0.80 per mile in the U.S. (when factoring fuel, insurance, depreciation, etc.), and hiring a ride (taxi or ride-share) often costs $1-$3 per mile, depending on the geography.​

These costs have kept certain use cases in check – for instance, few people would take a 50-mile daily commute by taxi, or have food delivered from a restaurant 30 miles away, because the economics don’t make sense.

Autonomy flips this equation on its head. Without a human driver to pay and with optimal (e.g. cars being in use potentially 24 hours a day as opposed to a fraction of that) utilization, the cost of an electric robo-taxi could drop to mere pennies per mile above electricity and maintenance expenses. Tesla has floated numbers around $0.30-$0.40 per mile to the end user for a robotaxi ride​, for instance.

When transportation becomes that inexpensive, we use more of it. Lower price points stimulate demand – a well-known economic effect. For instance, if a trip across town costs $3 instead of $15, people will take more trips, and they’ll be willing to travel farther for the same activities.

What might this look like in daily life?

We could see new use cases bloom: families might opt to not own a second car, instead relying on robo-taxis for occasional needs (thus expanding the ride-hail market). Individuals might live further from city centers if commuting in an autonomous pod lets them work or relax during the ride – turning dreaded traffic time into productive or leisure time.

On-demand mobility could reach communities and demographics currently underserved by transit. Even something as simple as running errands could change: your autonomous car could drop you at the store entrance and go park itself or go pick up another family member, then return when summoned – saving time and parking hassle.

Changes in Logistics and Delivery

Autonomy isn’t just about moving people. Moving goods autonomously is equally transformative. We are starting to see pilot programs for autonomous delivery vans, sidewalk robots, and even drones for last-mile delivery.

The economics of e-commerce could shift further in favor of delivery over in-person shopping when human labor is less of a limiting factor. For instance, today a significant cost of grocery delivery is the driver’s time; autonomous delivery bots or vehicles could make it feasible to get a pint of ice cream delivered at midnight for a trivial fee.

The trucking industry, faced with chronic driver shortages, is also eager for solutions.

Autonomous trucks could operate almost nonstop (only pausing for fuel/charge and maintenance), drastically shortening delivery times cross-country. A truck that can drive 20+ hours a day could nearly double the output of today’s truck that is limited by human hours-of-service rules. This means faster shipping and potentially lower freight costs, which in turn lowers costs of goods for consumers. And ultimately cheaper goods and services.

Rethinking Consumer Behavior

As autonomy reduces the “cost” of distance – both in money and in mental effort – consumers may change behaviors in subtle ways. We might see more cross-town dining and entertainment if getting there is cheap and easy. Package returns or store pickups (think curbside) become more convenient when your car can run errands without you.

Families could send an autonomous vehicle to shuttle kids to activities safely, without a parent having to leave work (a concept many parents would welcome!). Car interiors might evolve to be more like offices or lounges since occupants are not driving; this could spawn an ecosystem of in-car services and entertainment.

In sum, mobility on demand may start to resemble an app utility, blending into our lives in ways we don’t even fully anticipate yet – much like how the smartphone’s always-on connectivity led to new habits (constant communication, on-demand everything).

Steve Jobs famously introduced the iPhone as a “an iPod, a phone, and an internet communicator” but even with his ambitious vision he fell short of the impact. It created entirely new industries, ride hailing among them, we see a similar potential here.

The common thread: when the price drops and convenience rises, usage expands.

Economic Productivity and Inclusion

On a macro scale, widespread autonomy could boost productivity.

Consider commuting: millions of hours are spent driving, which is essentially dead time in terms of work output (and often not very relaxing either). If those hours are freed up – people can work, read, or rest during commutes – that’s a gain for productivity or well-being. Moreover, mobility-as-a-service could serve populations unable to drive (elderly, disabled) far better than current options, increasing their access to commerce and social life.

We could see higher labor force participation in some segments if commuting or transportation is no longer a barrier (imagine someone visually impaired gaining independent mobility via AV services).

From Autonomous Cars to Autonomous Everything

The lessons and technologies of self-driving cars (sensors, AI decision-making, robotics) are spilling over into other domains.

We’re entering an age where autonomy will not be limited to cars. Drones are achieving autonomy in the skies (for inspection, delivery, agriculture monitoring). Warehouse robots (more on that in the next section) autonomously ferry goods within fulfillment centers. Factories are increasingly using autonomous mobile robots to transport parts. Even in software, “autonomy” in the form of AI agents could handle tasks for us without constant direction.

The common thread is AI taking over repetitive or dangerous tasks, and working tirelessly. This has broad economic implications: it could lead to higher output with less input, which is the definition of productivity growth. Some worry about job displacement, and indeed certain job categories (e.g. taxi and truck drivers) will [MC1] eventually decline.

While there is a chance this time is different, always dangerous words, the Luddites have been consistently wrong. History shows technology creates new jobs even as it displaces old ones – often jobs that are higher skilled or entirely new industries. We believe the net effect of autonomy will be to boost economic growth.

Rise of the Humanoid Robots

If autonomous cars represent AI seeping into how we move around, humanoid robots represent AI coming to life in a physical form to help us with work.

This is no longer science fiction.

Over the last 18 months, we’ve seen a proliferation of prototypes and startups focused on general-purpose, human-like robots. Tesla’s bipedal robot Optimus (also known as the Tesla Bot) grabbed headlines when it was first revealed in 2022, but it’s far from alone now.

Companies like Figure AI, Agility Robotics, Apptronik, Sanctuary AI, and others have all made significant strides with humanoid designs. Many are chasing the ultimate holy grail of products a workable, intelligent, and economic labor supply. We are witnessing the dawn of a new industry – one that aims to build robots that can work in our factories, warehouses, and even offices and homes someday.

In Q1 2025, Nightview’s research team did a deep dive on this emerging field, attended demos and spoke with former executives at some of these companies. Our goal here is to understand who’s leading, what use cases are emerging first, and how big the opportunity might be.

First, let’s clarify why so many are pursuing a humanoid form factor.

The human environment (buildings, tools, infrastructure) is designed around the human shape and abilities. A robot that can eventually navigate our world with similar agility and use the same tools as we do (handle a drill, carry a box, climb stairs, etc.) has a near-infinite range of potential tasks.

Rather than designing a special robot for every job (one for welding, another for dishwashing, another for elderly care), a general-purpose humanoid could, in theory, be trained or instructed to do many different tasks, much like a person can learn new skills.

This is the long-term vision: “robot workers” that can take over mundane or hazardous jobs and augment human labor in countless ways. It sounds fantastical, but so did self-driving cars at one point – and now we see those on the cusp of reality. The pace of progress in robotics (thanks to advances in AI, sensors, and actuators) has accelerated, and importantly the cost to build these robots is coming down dramatically (as noted, cheaper components and better designs).

Even under more conservative assumptions, the TAM is huge; for example, even a 10% penetration of manufacturing tasks by humanoids would imply hundreds of billions in robot sales. And that’s just on a replacement level of current labor we ultimately see this as *increasing* the overall workforce dramatically.

That’s the opportunity.
