data.title: Nebius: Poised For A Game Changing Q1
data.description:
My Past Coverage

I rated Nebius Group N.V. (NASDAQ:NBIS) as a "Strong Buy" back in January 2025, as I saw it falling on DeepSeek news at the time for absolutely no reason. Unfortunately for my coverage history, I updated my thinking and bullish thesis on the stock right at its peak in late February, reviewing its Q4 FY2024 quarterly results. The market didn't agree with me on Q4 and punished Nebius for its sales miss at the time, sending its stock price below $20/share (from $45-50/share in late February). I averaged down myself and am quite happy about it now, as NBIS started to recover rapidly, following Nvidia's (NVDA) example. Since my coverage initiation, the stock is up by almost 19% while the S&P 500 index (SPX) (SP500) (SPY) is down by about 2.5%:

My Thesis Update Before Nebius Group's Q1 Print

The company said in late April that they'll report Q1 2025 figures on May 20th (before market open), and I think the recent strength that we see in the stock's price action is just a prerequisite for a subsequent bullish move.

I still see the management's targeted $750 million to $1 billion in ARR by December 2025, and I expect at least $220 million in ARR as of the March quarter. What creates a real massive buying opportunity is that we haven't seen any positives from Wall Street's revisions regarding Nebius Group's top line in the next few years—the firm's odds of beating have increased dramatically, in my view. So based on all that, I reiterate my “Strong Buy” on NBIS stock, and I am looking forward to seeing what the management actually managed to achieve for fiscal Q1 2025.

My Updated Reasoning

I already did a walk-through of Nebius Group's results for Q4 FY2024. In a nutshell, they had revenues of $37.9 million, which was below Street's consensus expectation of $58.1 million, so it was a huge top-line miss of >34%, which drove the stock price down by over 60% in a matter of weeks.

On a positive side, Nebius managed to raise $700 million during Q4 from esteemed investors like Nvidia and Accel, bolstering their cash balance up to $2.4 billion—the room for growth became much bigger. And that growth isn't hypothetical as they plan to continue their aggressive expansion of data center footprint in both the U.S. (in Kansas City) and Europe (in Finland, France, and a new H200 cluster in Iceland). In addition to all that, they launched a new AI cloud platform and the “AI Studio” Inference-as-a-Service offering, at the same time expanding their global sales and marketing teams (mainly in the U.S.).

As many investors remain concerned about the slowing demand for DCs, I think we should look at the hard actual data first, and it's telling a completely different story. The demand side is very strong as Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), and Meta Platforms (META) spent ~$217 billion in 2024 alone and are committing >$320 billion in 2025. OpenAI, Softbank, and Oracle (ORCL) are going to inject ~$500 billion into advanced AI infrastructure across the U.S. over the next 4 years. Most of the investments are heavily training-focused, meaning that training needs in deployed facilities, once those facilities are built, will likely decline in 5–10 years, but I think this will be offset by big tech's need to keep investing in AI because the inference needs will rise. This is where Nebius's development of “AI Studio” and its Inference-as-a-Service offerings comes into play: They seemingly started off early, not just solely betting their future on an unending, linear growth of large-scale training demand, but trying to diversify and remain sustainable as the DC market “penetrates” further.

Also, where Nebius's custom full-stack approach shines is in its in-house design of DCs, servers, and cooling solutions across different continents. I believe that by building high-quality, adaptable DCs in strategic locations, Nebius is creating “sticky” assets so that once their customers deploy on their platform, they are less likely to migrate due to the inherent costs and complexities involved.

As it's becoming a major topic among growth investors since CoreWeave (CRWV) IPO'd in late March this year, I should discuss the reason why I'd pick Nebius Group right now. First of all, NBIS is designing its own hardware and data centers, and its business structure is much better diversified across multiple high-growth niches. Second, unlike CoreWeave's balance sheet, Nebius is basically debt-free right now. Third, with no intention of lowering the qualifications of CRWV's engineers, Nebius Group's team, largely hailing from Yandex, seems to be top-notch in the tech space in general, and they are likely not as costly as CRWV's America-based staff.

Now let's talk about the Q1 expectations from Nebius. According to Seeking Alpha premium data, NBIS's Q1 revenue should come close to $57.73 million, which would be a sequential increase of >50%.

Let's keep in mind that Nebius is in a rapid ramp-up phase, and their "$220 million March ARR figure" provided during the Q4 earnings call looks like a powerful leading indicator, which eventually didn't impact the consensus in any positive way. Everyone was focused on the Q4 top line huge miss, and Wall Street analysts decided to lower their estimates despite the above leading indicator suggesting the opposite direction for the firm's revenues.

The company is targeting 100MW of operational capacity by year-end 2025, which seems doable with all those facilities in New Jersey, Finland, France, and Iceland. Also, the deployment of NVIDIA's H200 GPUs is ongoing, and the highly anticipated Blackwell GPUs (B200 in Q2, GB200 in Q3) are on the horizon, which management expects to be a “main source” of future income. So I don't see any reason to remain as skeptical about the next few quarters' revenues as the current consensus figures suggest.

Furthermore, while Nebius is currently loss-making on a GAAP basis (which is obvious), the path towards EBITDA breakeven, which management guided could occur at some point during 2025, looks visible to me, unless the Q1 print proves me wrong on this point.

As we're coming closer to the earnings release date (May 20, 2025), it's crucial to assess the firm's valuation. NBIS trades at 8.86x forward price-to-sales, which seems much higher compared to CRWV's multiple of 2.7x:

But again, we have to keep in mind here that a) Nebius Group's forward sales is based on consensus, which is likely underestimated, b) NBIS seems to be better diversified across geographic regions and business structure in general, and c) NBIS should grow at a faster CAGR thanks to the previous points and also leaning on its much lower base both in terms of sales and earnings.

I think that a fair P/S ratio for the level of growth investors are going to get in the next few years should be close to 11-13x right now. If so, NBIS is undervalued by ~40% by the end of 2025 (if the $1 billion ARR comes by the year-end). For a longer period of holding, the upside is much higher—I think it will be measured in factors once Nebius executes on its strategic plans. I'm not willing to sell any time soon, so I'll leave my “Strong Buy” in place.

Risks And Takeaway

I admit that I may be wrong in my overall assessment of Nebius Group's competitive standing and role in AI infrastructural build-out developments. Skeptics might argue that NBIS is still in its early innings of growth, and so it's simply too early to make any definitive conclusions regarding those billions in potential revenues in the upcoming years. They're undertaking a massive and rapid global expansion of their data center footprint and operations, and since it's a costly process, they'll likely need to raise more capital eventually. So if it's done publicly, the dilution might cause shareholder value erosion, and in this case, the fair value would be much lower than today's market cap.

Anyway, I think that the upcoming Q1 print should be a real game changer for Nebius and the market's perception of what it's capable of. Last time they reported, the Street didn't understand the top-line miss and punished the stock to depressed levels. This time, I think everything should be different because of higher odds of beating following the ongoing progress in expanding operations, and at the same time, a lowering of the Street's consensus estimates. I reiterate my “Strong Buy”, waiting to see NBIS ~40% higher by the year's end.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: MCHI: A Buy If You Believe In De-Escalation
data.description:
Recently, I was asked by one of the editors at Seeking Alpha if I had any updated thoughts on the iShares MSCI China ETF (NASDAQ:MCHI). Most recently, I was the lone analyst with a bullish rating on the MCHI ETF (Figure 1).

To be fair, my bullish article on the MCHI ETF was written in October, prior to Donald Trump winning the American presidency and unleashing his Trump 2.0 agenda. With so many moving parts in the bilateral relationship between the U.S. and China, here are my latest thoughts on the MCHI ETF.

Brief Fund Overview

First, for those not familiar, the iShares MSCI China ETF is the largest China-focused ETF with over $6 billion in assets. MCHI gives investors a convenient way to invest in mid- and large-cap Chinese companies by tracking the MSCI China Index ("Index"), an index that measures the performance of the top 85% of market capitalization of the Chinese equity markets. The MCHI ETF charges a 0.59% expense ratio (Figure 2).

Trump 2.0 Unleashed Trade War With China

In my opinion, the biggest macroeconomic development in the past few months has been the re-election of Donald Trump as the President of the United States. Trump campaigned on a broad platform of 'bringing down inflation', protecting America's borders from illegal immigrants, and enacting tariffs against 'unfair trading partners'.

While the jury is still out about Trump's performance on inflation and immigration, the second Trump administration has certainly started with a 'bang' on trade. As soon as he was inaugurated in January, President Trump started to threaten Canada, Mexico, and China with tariffs in response to a perceived 'fentanyl crisis'.

Chinese Stocks Rode the Trump Rollercoaster

Initially, Chinese stock markets were relatively sanguine, as the tariff rates on China (10% initial fentanyl tariffs, increased to 20% in February) were actually less than the 25% rate threatened against America's closest ally, Canada (Figure 3). The MCHI ETF made a local high on March 19th, as Trump's attention appeared to be focused on Canada.

Liberating Investors From Their Money

Then, on April 2nd, President Trump unleashed 'reciprocal tariffs' on almost every sovereign nation in the world at his much-touted Liberation Day event (Figure 4). From China at 34% to the United Kingdom at 10%, no country was spared from his 'reciprocal tariffs'.

China, in an act of defiance, retaliated against President Trump's 'reciprocal tariffs' and imposed 34% tariffs of their own against American-made goods.

President Trump raised the ante on April 8th by increasing the Chinese tariffs by 50%, to 'punish' China for retaliating. China responded in kind on April 9th. The tit-for-tat escalation ultimately reached unfathomable proportions, as U.S. tariffs on Chinese-made goods reached 145% and Chinese tariffs on American-made goods reached 125% (Figure 5).

This effectively created a trade embargo between the U.S. and China, since no importer could reasonably bear the cost of the tariffs.

Chinese stocks and the S&P 500 Index fell in sympathy, with the MCHI ETF plunging by 16.5% from April 2nd to April 8th, and the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust falling by 12.1% (Figure 6).

America Reached a Pain Point

However, faced with plunging stock and bond markets, and a possible steep recession (Figure 7), President Trump quickly backed down and reversed his position on 'reciprocal tariffs'. On Wednesday, April 9th, he announced a blanket 90-day pause on the application of the 'reciprocal tariff' rates, except for Chinese tariffs and a 10% baseline tariff.

The Threat of Empty Shelves Brought U.S. And China to the Negotiating Table

Furthermore, as the trade war with China dragged on, American businessmen and politicians became increasingly worried about empty shelves beginning in June, as the number of containerships from China had plummeted due to the tariff uncertainty.

In a surprise move, American Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent agreed to meet with Chinese officials in Geneva, Switzerland, on May 10th in hopes of 'de-escalating' the situation.

Fortunately, saner heads prevailed, and Secretary Bessent was able to secure a 90-day reduction in China's retaliatory tariffs to 10%, while American tariffs on Chinese goods were reduced to 30%. This de-escalation of the trade war reignited global stocks, and both the MCHI ETF and the SPY ETF are now trading higher than they were before Trump's Liberation Day (Figure 6 above).

Buy MCHI If You Are Bullish on U.S.-China Trade Relations

So, after the abbreviated history of Trump's tariffs above, what is my current outlook for the MCHI ETF and Chinese stocks in general?

In my opinion, the MCHI ETF has now become a binary bet on the ultimate resolution of the trade war with China.

On the upside, President Trump appears to be chastened by market volatility in the past month and has sounded more conciliatory about China. Even before Secretary Bessent secured the de-escalation, President Trump had floated an 'ultimate' Chinese tariff rate far below 145%.

On the downside, President Trump is notoriously mercurial, and the trade situation could deteriorate over the next 3 months. If China and the U.S. are not able to negotiate a lasting trade deal, negative sentiment could resume towards Chinese stocks, and the MCHI ETF could suffer.

However, in my opinion, it may be easier to stimulate demand in China compared to creating supply in the U.S. Even before the trade détente, China was getting ready to announce stimulus measures such as lower interest rates and fiscal 'cash for clunkers' stimulus programs. On the other hand, despite President Trump announcing trillions in 'investments' in America, it will likely take many years before production capacity is built up in the U.S.

This means China 'has the cards' in the short term, while the U.S. may have the upper hand in the long run.

Valuation Remains Compelling

Taking a step back, I continue to believe the MCHI ETF is a better relative buy compared to the SPY ETF due to valuations. For example, the MSCI China Index currently has a Fwd P/E of 10.8x (Figure 8).

This is almost half the valuation of the S&P 500 Index, which trades at a 20.4x Fwd P/E (Figure 9).

If the trade talks break down, I fear the SPY ETF has more valuation downside compared to the MCHI ETF. Alternatively, if the trade dispute with the U.S. is resolved amicably, I believe the valuation overhang on Chinese stocks could be removed, and there is more valuation upside with MCHI.

While cheap valuations do not have to translate into stock rallies, it does provide investors with a 'margin of safety' and help me sleep better at night.

Risk to MCHI

The biggest risk to the MCHI ETF and Chinese stocks remains short-term volatility from the U.S.-China trade war. In my opinion, investors should 'buy the dip' and not chase short-term returns.

While stocks have ripped higher in the past few days, I'm confident there will be setbacks that will present investors with buying opportunities over the coming months as both the U.S. and China want to negotiate the best outcome for their respective countries.

Conclusion

A lot has happened in the past few months since I last reviewed the MCHI ETF. Most importantly, President Trump's trade war with China has turned the MCHI ETF into a binary bet on the bilateral trade relationship in the short term.

For those bullish on this relationship, I believe the MCHI ETF is a fantastic buy. If the two nations can resolve their trade differences, the MCHI ETF, at 10.8x Fwd P/E, has a lot of room to run.

On the other hand, if trade talks break down, Chinese stocks may find valuation support sooner, compared to the SPY ETF trading at 20.4x Fwd P/E. I continue to rate MCHI a buy and prefer it compared to expensive American stocks.

data.title: Attractive Valuation And Low Debt Make Virtus A Compelling Case
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Gator Capital Management Q1 2025 Investor Letter.

We believe Virtus Investment Partners (NYSE:VRTS) offers a compelling investment from current levels. Virtus Investment Partners is a multi-boutique traditional investment manager. The company has several investment affiliates with their own investment teams and strategies. Virtus provides centralized sales, middle- and back-office services to support these teams. Virtus has grown by using cash flow to acquire additional investment firms. It keeps the investment teams of the acquisitions in place and centralizes sales and operations. We successfully owned Virtus for the Fund several years ago.

Our investment thesis is below:

  1. High-Quality Business Due to Recurring Revenue and High Free Cash Flow - Virtus Investment Partners boasts a high-quality business model underpinned by recurring revenue streams and elevated free cash flow. The firm's strategic focus on asset management ensures steady income through management fees, which are largely predictable and stable. This recurring revenue is a testament to the company's robust client relationships and consistent performance, providing a strong foundation for continued growth. Furthermore, high free cash flow enables Virtus to reinvest in its business, pursue acquisitions, and return capital to shareholders, enhancing overall value.
  2. Low Valuation on Price to Earnings and EV/EBITDA Basis - VRTS has an attractive valuation. It trades for 6.5x price-to-earnings (P/E) and a shockingly low 3.0x on Enterprise Value to Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization (EV/EBITDA) basis. VRTS has a strong balance sheet that matches the strong cash flow profile of the business. The company only has $100 million of net debt, which is about 0.3x EBITDA. In addition, VRTS carries a portfolio of seed investments in start-up funds and products valued at $140M. It recycles this seed capital into incubating new funds and products as existing seed investments payoff or fail. Virtus also has about $140M in CLO equity investments. These investments are made as its advisors form new CLOs. We subtract the seed investments and the CLO equity investments from Enterprise Value when we quote the 3.0x EV/EBITDA multiple. One could argue that seed investments and CLO equity investments are necessary to run the business. If we excluded these two items from Enterprise Value because they are necessary to run the business, we still calculate a very low 4.0x EV/EBITDA multiple. Virtus trades at a lower multiple compared to its peers.
  3. Low Debt Levels Provide Management Flexibility - The firm's prudent approach to debt management further strengthens its investment appeal. Virtus Investment Partners maintains low debt levels, which afford management significant flexibility to act on M&A or share repurchase opportunities. This conservative financial stance mitigates risk and enhances the firm's ability to sustain operations during economic downturns.
  4. Potential for Earnings Accretive Acquisition - Virtus Investment Partners is well-positioned to pursue earnings accretive acquisitions. The firm's strong balance sheet and free cash flow provide the necessary capital to acquire additional investment management capabilities. Multiples for privately held investment management businesses are inexpensive. Virtus has a strong platform and history of integrating acquisitions to realize synergies and create value.
  5. Consistent Dividends and Stock Repurchases – VRTS management has been consistent in returning capital to shareholders through dividends and stock repurchases. VRTS stock currently yields 5.6%. Stock repurchases further enhance shareholder value by reducing outstanding shares and increasing earnings per share (EPS). In Q1, VRTS management repurchased about 2% of the stock and signaled that they would be more aggressive given the stock price decline from early March. We would encourage management to consider increasing leverage by 1.0x EBITDA and repurchase 30% of the shares.

There are risks to our investment thesis on Virtus:

  1. Challenged Flows Due to Active Management Out of Favor - Despite its strengths, Virtus Investment Partners faces challenges in asset flows, primarily due to the growing trend towards passive management. Active management has fallen out of favor with some investors, who prefer the lower fees and perceived simplicity of passive strategies. This shift poses a headwind for Virtus, potentially impacting on its ability to attract and retain assets. The firm must navigate this evolving landscape by demonstrating the value of active management and differentiating its offerings through superior performance and client service.
  2. Some Past Acquisitions May Not Have Paid Off - Virtus's acquisition strategy, while generally successful, has not been without its missteps. Some past acquisitions have not delivered the anticipated returns. These underperforming acquisitions can weigh on financial results and erode investor confidence.
  3. Waiting for Flows to Improve or Management to Take Action - Virtus Investment Partners is at a crossroads, awaiting improvement in asset flows or action from management to make an acquisition or materially increase share repurchases. This state of waiting creates uncertainty and requires patience from investors.

In summary, Virtus Investment Partners presents a compelling investment case with a robust business model, attractive valuation, low debt levels, and a clear commitment to shareholder returns. However, the firm must navigate challenges related to asset flows and past acquisitions. Investors should weigh these strengths and weaknesses carefully, considering Virtus's potential for growth and the strategic actions needed to address its current hurdles. With prudent management and strategic execution, Virtus Investment Partners could deliver substantial value to its shareholders over the long term.

data.title: U.S. To Impose Tariffs In 2-3 Weeks As Administration 'Lacks Capacity' To Negotiate With Everyone
data.description:
Overview

While the US dollar is mostly a little softer today against the G10 currencies, it remains mostly within Wednesday's range. The yen is a notable exception. It made a new high for the week despite the contraction in Q1 '25 GDP. Most emerging market currencies are firmer. The markets have not reacted to a statement from President Trump that his administration lacks the capacity to negotiate with all the parties, so in the next 2-3 weeks, the US will simply set the tariff rates.

Asia-Pacific equities ended the week on a mixed note. Europe's STOXX 600 (STOXX) is up about 0.6%, the fourth daily advance this week. US index futures are up about 0.25%. European bonds are rallying. The 10-year benchmark yields are off 4-5 bp today. The US 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) is down almost four basis points to slip below 4.40%. Note that the ECB's pressure on its member banks to reduce dollar funding needs could lead to sales of USD assets, but the impact has been offset by signals that the US will lower the Supplemental Leverage Ratio, which will incentivize banks to hold more Treasuries. Gold staged an impressive recovery yesterday, rallying from $3120 to $3240. Follow-through buying lifted it to around $3252 today, where sellers drove it back through $3200. It is hovering near there in late European morning turnover. July WTI is consolidating within yesterday's broad range and is trading mostly between $60.80 and $61.80 today.

USD: The Dollar Index is in a narrow range (~100.50-80) and remains inside Wednesday's range (~100.25-101.15) for the second consecutive session. Today's data do not have much market-moving ability. This includes housing starts and permits, NY Fed services survey, and the March portfolio capital report (TIC). After an 11.2% plunge in March housing starts, a modest recovery is expected. Perhaps the market may be most sensitive to the preliminary May University of Michigan consumer confidence survey and inflation expectations. Consumer expectations may have improved a little. The one-year inflation expectation stood at a lofty level, even though it seemed exaggerated at 6.5% in March. The 5–10-year inflation outlook was at 4.4%. In comparison, the two-year breakeven (difference between the inflation protected security and the conventional note) is about 2.65%, while the 5-10 year breakevens are around 2.35-2.40%. Meanwhile, the month-over-month increase in the April CPI was a smidgeon less than expected, and the PPI actually fell. In nine of the 10 sessions through Wednesday, the market downgraded the chances of a September cut. However, odds increased after yesterday's soft retail sales (decline in the control measure, which excludes autos, gasoline, building materials and food services) and contraction in manufacturing output. A week ago, the Fed funds futures market had priced in about 66 bp of cuts this year, and now a little less than 57 bp. Once again, the market deviated from the Fed's guidance and has converged again with it, rather than the other way around. Turning to the TIC data, many column inches have been recorded this year discussing capital flight from the US. Yet, note that in the Jan-Feb period, the TIC data showed a net inflow of about $338 billion, compared with a net outflow of around $59 billion. In March 2024, the US recorded a net inflow of almost $98 billion. The concern became acute in April, for which the TIC data will not be available until mid-June.

EURO: The euro has been confined to about a two-cent range this week between around $1.1065 and $1.1265. It is trading today in about a 20-tick range on either side of $1.12, where options for nearly 4 billion euros expire today. The eurozone reported a March trade surplus of 36.8 billion euros, widening from the February 24 billion euro surplus. This puts the Q1 surplus at about 61.6 billion euros. The surplus in Q1 '24 was around 58.2 billion euros. Some observers see a seasonal pattern favoring a wider surplus in March, but in the last 20 years, the surplus widening in 13 March's. That said, the surplus has widened in eight of the past 10 March's.

CNY: The dollar's recovery from the low for the year set Tuesday near CNH7.1790 has been stymied near CNH7.2160. The greenback is has remained within Wednesday's range (~CNH7.19-7.2160). The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at CNY7.1938. (CNY7.1963 yesterday and CNY7.2095 a week ago). It was set lower for the fourth time this week. Many observers, including ourselves, think that China came out ahead in last week's trade talks. The US dropped (for 90 days) the reciprocal tariffs announced in early April, as Beijing required to hold the talks. Still, the sectoral tariffs, the de minimis tariffs (54% instead of 120% and $100 fee), and the planned port charges remain in effect. Taking advantage of the 90-cooling off period has seen a surge in container bookings from China to the US, pushing up shipping costs. Walmart (WMT) indicated that despite the lower tariffs, it plans on raising prices shortly. Meanwhile, the US has stepped up its efforts to block other countries from taking Huawei chips. India appears to have been emboldened by China and is threatening to retaliate for what it says are really "safeguard" tariffs and not really national security protections in the steel and aluminum tariffs. Meanwhile, the US administration says a trade deal with India may be near. Brazil and China signed over around 30 sectoral and investment agreements with China this week, and Colombia has reportedly signed a memorandum of understanding (MOU) with China on the Belt Road Initiative. In the region, Venezuela, Ecuador, Chile, Peru, Bolivia, and Panama have formally joined the BRI.

JPY: After rallying to JPY148.65 on Monday, its highest level since April 3, the dollar has unwound the gains and set a new low for the week today slightly below JPY145.00. There are options for $735 million that expire today. The four-day decline has seen the greenback retrace (38.2%) of its gains since the eight-month low was recorded on April 22 near JPY139.90. The (50%) retracement is near JPY144.25. Counterintuitively, the yen's gains came despite a disappointing Q1 GDP. Japan reported a 0.2% contraction in Q1 GDP after a 0.6% expansion in Q4 '24. Consumption was flat after Q4 consumption was revised from zero to 0.1%. Business spending jumped 1.4% after a 0.8% increase in Q4 (initially a 0.6% increase). Inventories contributed 0.3% after dragging GDP lower by 0.3% in Q4. Net exports cut growth by 0.8% after adding 0.7% in Q4 '24. On a more positive note, March industrial production was revised to 0.2% from an initial estimate of a 1.1% decline. The swaps market has about 16 bp of tightening discounted for this year, down a few basis points from this week's high.

GBP: Sterling continues to consolidate inside Wednesday's range (~$1.3255-1.3360). The down trendline drawn off the recent highs comes in near $1.3360 today. A move above there would reset the sights on $1.3400-45 area. On the other hand, a break of the $1.3225-50 area could signal a return to the $1.3140 area. Although yesterday's first reading of Q1 GDP was better than expected (0.7%) and the strongest since Q1 '24 (0.9%), which itself was the best since the end of 2021, the momentum may have stalled as the quarter wound down. Attention next week turns to Q2 data, with April CPI, retail sales, and the preliminary May PMI. The swaps market sees little chance of back-to-back rate cuts, which nearly rules out a cut next month. The market shaved the odds of a cut in August to about 72% from nearly 97% at the end of last week.

CAD: For the third time this week, the greenback was greeted by sellers when it poked above CAD1.40. Chart resistance is seen in the CAD1.4015-20 area. It settled near CAD1.3960 yesterday and was sold to about CAD1.3935 before finding new bids. Still, the momentum indicators are still moving higher, and this suggests that despite the frustration, there is scope for another try higher. However, a break of the CAD1.3890-1.3900 area would weaken the technical outlook. Canada also reports its March portfolio flows today. In the first two months of the year, Canada reported a net inflow of C$1.5 billion. In the January-February 2024 period, Canada recorded net inflows of C$9.2 billion. In March 2024, Canada saw C$14.7 billion in capital inflows.

AUD: After being turned back from a slight push above $0.6500 on Wednesday, the Australian dollar fell a little below nearly $0.6390 yesterday. That has held today, and the Aussie has recovered to about $0.6435. The momentum indicators have only recently turned lower, suggesting scope for further Aussie losses. We suspect that the downside correction can continue and target the $0.6300 area. There is little doubt in the market's mind that the Reserve Bank of Australia will cut its cash target rate next week by 25 bp to 3.85%. Moreover, the market is confident of another cut in Q3 and Q4.

MXN: As widely expected, Mexico's central bank cut its overnight target rate by another 50 bp to 8.50%. It is the third cut this year and the seventh consecutive cut. The central bank is not done and suggested that "more adjustments of a similar magnitude" were possible. This is a bit more dovish than we and the market expected. The US dollar made new highs for the session on the announcement to nearly MXN19.52 but has come back offered today, returning to the MXN19.4500 area in the European morning. The swaps market has almost another 125 bp of Banxico cuts discounted over the next 12 months. The peso has appreciated by about 7% this year, of which a little more than 5% took place this quarter.

Original Post
